MOTION TO STRIKE
Off Calendar Dec-03-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE): Atty. WALLACE W. HAMMONS, Esq. of Hammons & Associates, Inc. (Tel: 310-606-5400) for defendant/cross-complainant/cross-defendant Milgard Manufacturing, Inc.;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFFS BRIDGE HOUSING CORPORATION, JAMES E ROBERTS-OBAYASHI CORPORATION, AND ARMSTRONG TOWNHOMES, LLC'S MOTION TO STRIKE.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiffs Armstrong Townhomes LLC and James E. Roberts-Obayashi Corp.'s motion to strike or tax Milguard Manufacturing, Inc.'s memorandum of costs is denied.

Plaintiffs sued Milgard for equitable indemnity and associated counts. Milgard made a CCP 998 offer for waiver of fees and costs. Plaintiffs did not accept, then lost Milgard's motion for judgment on the pleadings - a loss that involuntarily dismissed Milgard and made it the prevailing party.

Plaintiffs fail to carry their burden to show that the CCP 998 was a bad-faith token offer. (Santantonio v. Westinghouse Broadcasting Co., Inc. (1994) 25 Cal.App.4th 102, 116-17.) The waiver of costs was worth a significant amount and this court found that "Milgard has de minimus exposure."

Filing & Motion Fees: Milgard is allowed its filing and motion fees. (CCP 1033.5 (a)(1).) Depositions: Milgard is allowed its deposition costs, including court reporter fees. (CCP 1033.5 (a)(11).) Subpoena Service: Milgard is allowed its costs for service of third-party subpoenas. (CCP 1033.5 (c)(1).) Expert Witnesses: The court exercises its discretion to grant Milgard post-CCP 998 expert fees. (Id. at (c)(1).) Court-Call: Telephonic participation in court proceedings is reasonably necessary during the Covid-19 pandemic.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)

Off Calendar Aug-18-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. WALLACE W. HAMMONS, Esq. of Hammons & Associates, Inc. (Tel: 310-606-5400) for defendant Milgard Manufacturing, Inc.
Atty. BILL H. KOLLIAS, Esq. of Prenovost, Normandin, Dawe & Rocha (Tel: 714-547-2444) for defendant Victaulic Company;

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFFS BRIDGE HOUSING CORPORATION, JAMES E ROBERTS-OBAYASHI CORPORATION, and ARMSTRONG TOWNHOMES, LLC's Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Milgard Manufacturing, Inc.S Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement Pursuant To C.C.P 877.6.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiffs' motion for reconsideration of the Court's July 14, 2021 ortder granting Milgard Manufacturing, Inc.'s motion for determination of good faith settlement pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 877.6 is denied.. Plaintiffs have failed to demonstrate diligence and provide a satisfactory explanation for failing to offer the purported new evidence at the original hearing. (In re Marriage of Herr (2009) 174 Cal.App.4th 1463, 1468; see also New York Times Co. v. Superior Court (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 206, 212; Garcia v. Hejmadi (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 674, 689-691.) As Milgard points out, Plaintiffs could have simply picked up the telephone and called the mediator before the prior hearing. Further, Milgard shows that Plaintiffs' portrayal of the facts is seriously deficient and incomplete. In any event, whether or not the settlement was reached with the assistance of an experienced mediator, there is no evidence of collusion, and Plaintiffs, who failed to submit any evidence in opposition to the original motion, do not meet their burden to show lack of good faith. (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 877.6(d). Milgard's request for sanctions is denied, because it did not file a separate motion for sanctions and did not comply with the 21-day safe harbor provision of Code of Civil Procedure section 128.5(f)(1)(B). (See CPF Vaseo Associates, LLC v. Gray (2018) 29 Cal.App.5th 997, 1002-1005 [explaining that section 128.5 was amended in 2017 to incorporate the safe harbor provisions of section 128.7]; In re Marriage of Sahafzadeh-Taeb & Taeb (2019) 39 Cal.App.5th 124, 128, 134 & fn. 2 [same].)

Order signed.

MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
Off Calendar Aug-02-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT MILGARD MANUFACTURING, INC.'S MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS.

APPEARANCES:
Bill H. Kollias, Esq. (714-547-2444; bkollias@pnbd.com) of Prenovost Normandin Dawe & Rocha for Defendant, Victaulic Company.
Sean Paden, Esq. (310-606-5400; spaden@hammonslegal.com) of Hammons & Associates, Inc. for Defendant, Milgard Manufacturing Inc. via Zoom.
John Black, Esq. for Defendant, P & M Window Company via Zoo.

DISCUSSED. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Milgard Manufacturing, Inc.'s motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted without leave to amend as to the fourth cause of action for breach of express warranty and the fifth cause of action for breach of implied warranty. The motion is moot as to the sixth cause of action for declaratory relief, which Plaintiff has dismissed.

The elements of breach of an express warranty are: " (1) an express warranty to repair defects given in connection with the sale of goods; (2) the existence of a defect covered by the warranty; (3) the buyer's notice to the seller of such a defect within a reasonable time after its discovery; (4) the seller's failure to repair the defect in compliance with the warranty; and (5) resulting damages." (Orichian v. BMW of North America, LLC (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 1322, 1333-34.) But before analyzing whether the elements of the claim are properly pled, privity of contract must be established as the preliminary prerequisite. (Windham at Carmel Mountain Ranch Assn. v. Superior Court (2003) 109 Cal.App.4th 1162, 1177.)

"The general rule is that privity of contract is required in an action for breach of either express or implied warranty and that there is no privity between the original seller and a subsequent purchaser who is in no way a party to the original sale." (Burr v. Sherwin Williams Co. (1954) 42 Cal.2d 682, 695.) While there are exceptions to this rule, none applies here. (Id. at 695-96.) It is clear on the face of the First Amended Complaint that there is no privity between the parties, as Plaintiff alleges that the windows were sold or distributed to it by another party, P&M Window Co. (See First Amend. Compl., at pars. 2, 14.) Plaintiff does not allege that it entered into a contract with Defendant. Because Plaintiff does not show any reasonable likelihood that it can overcome the defect, the motion is granted without leave to amend.

Prevailing/Moving party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS-DEFENDANT JOHNSON CONTROLS, INC.'s DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT:

Cross-defendant Johnson Controls, Inc.'s demurrer to Plaintiffs' second amendment to first amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to the fourth and fifth causes of action for breach of express and implied warranties. "The general rule is that privity of contract is required in an action for breach of either express or implied warranty and that there is no privity between the original seller and a subsequent purchaser who is in no way a party to the original sale." (Burr v. Sherwin Williams Co. (1954) 42 Cal.2d 6892, 695; Windham at Carmel Mountain Ranch Assn. v. Superior Court (2003) 109 Cal.App.4th 1162, 1169.) While numerous exceptions apply to this general rule, Plaintiffs do not plead any facts showing contractual privity, status as an intended third-party beneficiary of the agreement, or a recognized exception to the privity rule. Plaintiffs make no argument in response. Plaintiffs' request in their opposition brief for leave to amend to name Tyco Fire Protection is improper. Plaintiffs must file a separate motion for leave to amend.
